items.myth: Naive Bayes assumes features are completely unrelated in the real world.
items.reality: It assumes features are independent **conditional on the class label y**. Features may correlate strongly in general; the simplification applies only inside each class-conditional model.
items.example: In spam detection, "FREE" and "!!!" co-occur often overall, but the model uses P(FREE|spam) and P(!!|spam) as separate factors given spam.

items.myth: The "naive" assumption means naive Bayes is always inferior to modern models.
items.reality: For high-dimensional sparse text and many tabular baselines, naive Bayes remains competitive, extremely fast, and often hard to beat without much more complexity.
items.example: Multinomial NB on bag-of-words frequently matches or beats linear SVMs on small and medium text datasets with minimal tuning.

items.myth: If conditional independence is violated, naive Bayes cannot classify correctly.
items.reality: Violated independence biases **calibrated probabilities** but argmax classification can still be accurate when class-conditional densities rank correctly—even with correlated features.
items.example: Gaussian NB on Iris often achieves >95% accuracy despite correlated sepal measurements; posteriors may be sharper or flatter than truth.

items.myth: Bayes' theorem requires the independence assumption.
items.reality: Bayes' theorem holds for any P(x|y). Independence is an optional modeling choice to factorize P(x|y)—not a requirement of Bayes' rule itself.
items.example: You could use a full joint P(x|y) with Bayes if you had enough data; naive Bayes trades fidelity for tractability.
